does: AutoRISE introduces a method for automated red-teaming of large language models that optimizes attack strategies themselves rather than just prompts within a fixed strategy. The approach uses a coding agent that iteratively edits executable attack programs—structured code that defines how attacks are generated and executed. An evaluation harness scores resulting attacks and provides per-example diagnostic feedback that guides the agent's subsequent edits. This enables structural modifications including new attack components and altered control flow logic. The authors release two benchmark suites created on disjoint target sets and evaluate across 11 models from five families using seven established jailbreak datasets. AutoRISE achieves 17.0 percentage point improvement in average attack success rate over the strongest baseline and shows particularly strong gains of up to 16 points on frontier models with initially low baseline success rates.
connects: This work extends prior automated red-teaming research that has focused on prompt optimization within human-designed attack frameworks. Earlier approaches like gradient-based prompt attack methods and prompt evolution techniques operate at the token or full-prompt level, treating the underlying attack strategy as fixed. AutoRISE builds on this tradition but shifts the optimization target from prompts to strategies themselves, drawing inspiration from program synthesis and automated algorithm design. The paper connects to broader work on jailbreaking and adversarial attacks against language models, incorporating insights from existing jailbreak datasets and evaluation methodologies. It also relates to agent-based code generation systems and in-context learning of coding agents, leveraging recent advances in LLMs' ability to write and edit code with feedback.
enables: This work opens avenues for discovering novel attack strategies that humans have not explicitly designed or anticipated, potentially revealing new vulnerabilities in language model safety mechanisms. The strategy-discovery framework could extend beyond red-teaming to other optimization domains where complex, structured solutions are needed. Future research could combine this approach with theoretical analysis to understand what structural properties make strategies effective, potentially informing more robust defense mechanisms. The method also enables adaptive red-teaming systems where attack strategies evolve in response to model updates or new defense techniques. Additional directions include scaling the approach to larger strategy spaces, integrating multi-objective optimization to balance attack effectiveness with realism and transferability, and applying similar program-synthesis techniques to other AI safety and security domains.
